did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning method, specifically a stacked sparse autoencoder (SSAE) combined with a softmax classifier, to automatically detect translucency in patches of clinical basal cell carcinoma (BCC) images. The model achieved 93% accuracy in this dermatology task.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a deep learning method (stacked sparse autoencoder) for automatic detection of translucency in clinical basal cell carcinoma images. It describes a specific dataset of 32 clinical images, details the methodology including patch extraction and model architecture, and reports original experimental results (93% accuracy) obtained via five-fold cross-validation. It is published in the Proceedings of APSIPA Annual Summit and Conference 2018.

title: Automatic detection of translucency using a deep learning method from patches of clinical basal cell carcinoma images
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated method using a stacked sparse autoencoder (SSAE) combined with a softmax classifier to detect translucency, a key clinical feature of basal cell carcinoma (BCC), from patches of clinical images. The dataset consisted of 32 clinical BCC images collected from the Vancouver Skin Care Centre, which were segmented into 4,401 non-overlapping patches by an expert dermatologist. The model was evaluated using a five-fold cross-validation approach on these patches. The proposed deep learning method achieved a detection accuracy of 93%, with a specificity of 97.1% and a sensitivity of 77%. These results suggest that the SSAE-based framework can effectively detect translucency in clinical BCC images, supporting its potential use in computer-aided diagnosis systems.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on clinical images of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) collected from 32 patients. The dataset consists of 32 clinical BCC images, all confirmed by histopathological examinations. The task is to detect translucency, a clinical feature of BCC, within patches of these images.

all_task_types.target: Presence of translucency in clinical basal cell carcinoma image patches
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task where the model predicts whether a patch contains translucency (label 1) or not (label 0). The method uses a stacked sparse autoencoder (SSAE) for feature extraction followed by a softmax classifier. The dataset consisted of 4,401 patches (797 translucent, 3,604 non-translucent). The model achieved 93% accuracy, 77% sensitivity, and 97.1% specificity via five-fold cross-validation.

datasets_used.name: Vancouver Skin Care Centre Clinical BCC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset consisting of 32 clinical images of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) collected from 32 patients at the Vancouver Skin Care Centre. The images were captured using a digital color camera in a contact-free manner and all cases were confirmed by histopathological examinations. The images were segmented by an expert dermatologist into regions of interest (ROI) and then divided into non-overlapping patches.

use.training: Used for training the stacked sparse autoencoder (SSAE) and softmax classifier via five-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for validation via five-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for testing via five-fold cross-validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the stacked sparse autoencoder (SSAE) and softmax classifier for translucency detection on the dataset of 4,401 patches.
To visualize and assess the diagnostic ability of the proposed method for detecting translucency.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and ROC curves via five-fold cross-validation.

Vancouver Skin Care Centre Clinical BCC.notes: Results were obtained using a five-fold cross-validation approach on 4,401 patches (797 translucent, 3,604 non-translucent) extracted from 32 clinical images. The Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve was also generated to visualize diagnostic ability.

architecture_used.main_model: Stacked Sparse Autoencoder (SSAE) combined with a Softmax Classifier
architecture_used.pretraining: Unsupervised layer-by-layer pretraining of sparse autoencoders; weights and biases initialized randomly

loss_function.autoencoder_loss: Mean square error + Weight decay + Kullback-Leibler divergence sparsity constraint

training_details.hardware: PC (Intel core i7 with 16GB RAM) and Geforce GTX NVIDIA Graphics processor Unit

classification_or_output_head.input: High-level features (h2) from the second layer of SSAE

preprocessing_techniques: Region of Interest (ROI) segmentation using bounding boxes by an expert dermatologist
Patch extraction: Non-overlapping patches of size 32 x 32 pixels extracted from ROI images
Input normalization: All three RGB color channels inputted simultaneously to the network
conclusions: The proposed stacked sparse autoencoder (SSAE) combined with a softmax classifier achieved a detection accuracy of 93%, a specificity of 97.1%, and a sensitivity of 77% for automatically detecting translucency in patches of clinical basal cell carcinoma (BCC) images.

The deep learning method demonstrated the ability to effectively detect translucency, a key clinical feature of BCC, from clinical images captured using a digital color camera in a contact-free manner.
The study suggests that the proposed SSAE-based framework has potential for use in computer-aided diagnosis systems for early detection of basal cell carcinoma.

The authors acknowledge that the current work is preliminary and plan to expand the method to infer translucency across entire skin lesions rather than just patches, with the ultimate goal of building a comprehensive computer-aided diagnosis system.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a very small dataset size, consisting of only 32 clinical images from a single center (Vancouver Skin Care Centre), which raises concerns about generalizability and external validation.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with 3,604 non-translucent patches compared to only 797 translucent patches, which may bias the model towards the majority class despite the high specificity.

The work is explicitly described as preliminary; the authors acknowledge that the method currently detects translucency only in patches rather than across entire skin lesions, limiting its direct clinical utility for whole-lesion diagnosis.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely using five-fold cross-validation on the internal dataset, and no independent test set or multi-center data was used.

The paper does not report any formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the reported metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity), limiting the statistical rigor of the evidence.

The method relies on manual segmentation of Regions of Interest (ROI) by an expert dermatologist to create bounding boxes before patch extraction, introducing potential human bias and limiting full automation.

The study focuses exclusively on clinical images captured by digital color cameras in a contact-free manner, distinguishing it from previous works that used dermoscopy images; however, this limits comparison with dermoscopy-based benchmarks.
